rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box. Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps
rendering_guide.creator_challenge: Student submission prompt — winners credited in course

parts.purpose: Hook — reactions are where bonds break and reform

blocks.body: Everything we've studied — atoms, electrons, bonds, formulas — has been about substances sitting still. Chemical reactions are where the action starts. Bonds break, atoms rearrange, and entirely new substances appear with completely different properties.

A chemical reaction is a process where starting substances (reactants) transform into new substances (products) through the breaking and forming of chemical bonds. The atoms don't change — they rearrange. Same Lego bricks, new structure.

You witness reactions constantly: a match striking (combustion), iron rusting (oxidation), bread rising (fermentation), food digesting (enzyme-catalyzed reactions). The chemical equation is the language we use to describe these transformations.

blocks.title: Reactions You Can See Right Now
blocks.body: Look at toast — the brown color is the Maillard reaction (amino acids reacting with sugars). An old penny's green patina is copper reacting with air. Every breath you exhale contains CO₂ — the product of glucose reacting with oxygen in your cells.

Try this: drop a fizzy antacid tablet in water. The bubbles are CO₂ gas being produced by a chemical reaction between the tablet's ingredients. Or put steel wool in vinegar — watch it dissolve over hours as iron reacts with acetic acid.

Signs of a chemical reaction: color change, gas bubbles, temperature change, precipitate formation (solid appearing in a liquid), or light/flame. These are your visual clues that bonds are breaking and reforming.

We've spent 13 lessons learning vocabulary — atoms, electrons, bonds, formulas, names. Now we write SENTENCES. A chemical equation is a complete sentence: 'These substances react to produce those substances.' Chemistry has grammar.

Every time you cook an egg, you're denaturing proteins. Every candle flame is combusting wax. Every breath converts glucose + oxygen into CO₂ + water + energy. You're a walking chemistry lab and you didn't even apply for the job.

The cool part: chemical equations have RULES. Atoms can't appear or disappear. What goes in must come out. Enforcing this rule is called 'balancing' — the most practiced skill in introductory chemistry.

parts.part_title: Conservation of Mass and Reading Equations
parts.purpose: Law of conservation of mass + how to read equations

blocks.title: Law of Conservation of Mass
blocks.body: In a chemical reaction, matter is neither created nor destroyed. Total mass of reactants equals total mass of products. Every atom that enters must exit — just rearranged.

blocks.title: Reading a Chemical Equation
blocks.body: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O

items.label: Left side = Reactants
items.body: 2H₂ + O₂ — the starting substances. Two molecules of hydrogen gas plus one molecule of oxygen gas.

items.label: Arrow = 'Yields'
items.body: The → means 'reacts to form' or 'produces.'

items.label: Right side = Products
items.body: 2H₂O — the new substances. Two molecules of water.

items.label: Big numbers = Coefficients
items.body: The 2 in front of H₂ and H₂O tells you HOW MANY molecules. Coefficients multiply everything in the formula.

blocks.title: Coefficients vs Subscripts
blocks.body: Coefficients (big numbers in front) = how many molecules. Subscripts (small numbers below) = how many atoms within each molecule. You can change coefficients to balance. You NEVER change subscripts — that changes the substance itself.

blocks.title: Atom Count Check
blocks.body: Left: 2H₂ = 4 H atoms. O₂ = 2 O atoms. Right: 2H₂O = 4 H + 2 O. Left = Right. Balanced.

blocks.prompt: Create a visual showing 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O using everyday objects (colored balls, fruit, Lego) to represent atoms. Show reactants on left, arrow, products on right. Make conservation of atoms visually obvious!

blocks.title: Build It With Objects
blocks.body: Get 4 objects of one color (H atoms) and 2 of another (O atoms). Group as reactants: two pairs of color-1 (two H₂) and one pair of color-2 (one O₂). Total: 6 objects.
Rearrange into products: two groups of 2 color-1 + 1 color-2 (two H₂O). Count: still 6. Same atoms, new arrangement. That's conservation of mass.
Lavoisier proved conservation of mass in 1789 by weighing sealed containers before and after reactions. Mass never changed. This one observation ended alchemy and launched modern chemistry.

blocks.title: Atoms Are Immortal (Chemically)
blocks.body: In reactions, atoms don't appear from nothing or vanish. They change partners. Like a dance — same people, different pairings. Nobody enters or leaves the ballroom.

The carbon atoms in your body were once inside a star, then CO₂ in the atmosphere, then in a plant, then in something that ate the plant, then your lunch. Those atoms have been recycled for 4.5 billion years. Conservation of mass means every atom in you has been somewhere else before.

CRITICAL: you can change COEFFICIENTS to balance, but NEVER change SUBSCRIPTS. Changing H₂O to H₂O₂ to 'get more oxygens' doesn't balance — it changes water into hydrogen peroxide. Different substance entirely.

questions.question: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, how many total hydrogen atoms on each side?
questions.explanation: Coefficients multiply all atoms. 2H₂ = 2×2 = 4 H. 2H₂O = 2×2 = 4 H. Equal — balanced.

questions.question: You burn 10g of wood and collect ALL ash and gases. Total mass of products?

options.A: Less than 10g — fire destroyed some mass
options.B: Exactly equal to mass of wood + oxygen consumed — mass is conserved

questions.explanation: Total reactant mass (wood + O₂ from air) = total product mass (ash + CO₂ + H₂O vapor + gases). Products seem lighter only because gases escape. If captured, mass is exactly conserved.

blocks.title: Why Balance?
blocks.body: An unbalanced equation violates conservation of mass. Balancing adjusts coefficients so every atom in reactants appears in products.

items.label: Step 1: Write the unbalanced equation
items.body: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃

items.label: Step 2: Count atoms on each side
items.body: Left: 1 Fe, 2 O. Right: 2 Fe, 3 O. Not balanced.

items.label: Step 3: Balance one element at a time
items.body: Fe: put 2Fe on left → 2Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃. Fe: 2=2 ✓. O: 2≠3.

items.label: Step 4: Find common multiples
items.body: O: LCM(2,3)=6. 3O₂ on left (6 O). 2Fe₂O₃ on right (6 O). Re-check Fe: 4 needed on left.

items.label: Step 5: Final
items.body: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃. Fe:4=4 ✓ O:6=6 ✓

items.label: C: 1=1 ✓
items.body: Already balanced.

items.label: H: 4 left → 2H₂O → 4H right ✓
items.body: Put 2 in front of H₂O.

items.label: O: right = 2+2 = 4 → 2O₂ left ✓
items.body: Put 2 in front of O₂.

items.label: Final
items.body: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. C:1=1 H:4=4 O:4=4 ✓

blocks.title: Balancing Rules
blocks.body: Only change COEFFICIENTS. NEVER change subscripts. Balance one element at a time. Save H and O for last. Always verify.

blocks.title: Balance These Yourself
blocks.body: Write on paper, count atoms, adjust coefficients, verify.

items.label: __ N₂ + __ H₂ → __ NH₃
items.hints: N: 2 left, 1 right → 2NH₃. H: 2 left, 6 right → 3H₂. Answer: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃.

blocks.body: Balancing is puzzle-solving — each coefficient constrains the others. Start with elements in fewest places, lock down, cascade. With practice, you'll see answers in seconds.

blocks.title: Atom Accounting
blocks.body: Balancing = accounting for atoms. Every atom on left must appear on right. No gains, no losses. If books don't balance, the equation is wrong.
It's like Sudoku for atoms. Write equation. Count. Doesn't match? Add coefficients until it does.

NEVER change subscripts to balance. Changing H₂O to H₂O₂ for 'more oxygen' is like telling a bank auditor you 'adjusted the account numbers.' That's not balancing — that's chemical fraud. H₂O₂ is hydrogen peroxide. The auditor is not amused.

questions.question: Balance: __ Al + __ O₂ → __ Al₂O₃
questions.explanation: Al: 4 left = 2×2 right. O: 3×2=6 left = 2×3=6 right. Option D changes subscript (forbidden).

questions.question: Balance: __ C₂H₆ + __ O₂ → __ CO₂ + __ H₂O
questions.explanation: C:4=4 H:12=12 O:14=14 ✓. Option B: O left=6, right=4+3=7. Doesn't balance.

questions.question: A student changes NaCl to NaCl₂ to balance Na + Cl₂ → NaCl. Why is this wrong?

options.A: NaCl₂ is valid but coefficients are better
options.B: Changing subscripts changes the substance — NaCl₂ doesn't exist. Only coefficients can change

questions.explanation: Subscripts define the compound. NaCl₂ doesn't exist (Na⁺ can't bond 2 Cl⁻). Correct: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl.

parts.purpose: Classify reactions: synthesis, decomposition, single/double replacement, combustion

blocks.title: Pattern Recognition
blocks.body: Almost all reactions fall into five patterns. Identifying the type lets you predict products from reactants.

items.label: Synthesis: A + B → AB
items.body: Two+ substances combine into ONE product. 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. Assembly.

items.label: Decomposition: AB → A + B
items.body: ONE substance breaks into simpler parts. 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂. CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. Breaking apart.

items.label: Single Replacement: A + BC → AC + B
items.body: One element swaps in for another. Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu. More reactive element displaces less reactive.

items.label: Double Replacement: AB + CD → AD + CB
items.body: Two compounds swap partners. NaCl + AgNO₃ → NaNO₃ + AgCl↓. Often produces precipitate or water.

items.label: Combustion: Fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O
items.body: Carbon compound burns in oxygen. CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. All burning of organic fuels.

blocks.prompt: Create a poster showing all 5 reaction types with consistent visual language — colored shapes for atoms, arrows for reactions. Make it wall-worthy! Best submission becomes the official reaction types diagram with your name.

blocks.title: Quick ID
blocks.body: One product → synthesis. One reactant → decomposition. Element + compound → single replacement. Two compounds swapping → double replacement. Fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O → combustion.

blocks.title: Find Each Type in Real Life

items.label: Synthesis
items.hints: Rust forming: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃. Iron + oxygen → one product.

items.label: Decomposition
items.hints: Baking soda in cookies: 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + H₂O + CO₂. Heat breaks it apart, CO₂ makes cookies rise.

items.label: Single Replacement
items.hints: Zinc nail in blue CuSO₄ solution. Zinc displaces copper — blue fades, copper coats the nail.

items.label: Double Replacement
items.hints: Baking soda + vinegar. Partners swap → CO₂ fizzes. The classic volcano experiment.

items.label: Combustion
items.hints: Burning a candle. Wax + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. Flame provides activation energy.

blocks.body: Every reaction you encounter can be classified into these five types. The classification tells you what to expect.

items.label: Synthesis: The Marriage
items.body: A + B → AB. Two become one. Wedding vibes.

items.label: Decomposition: The Divorce
items.body: AB → A + B. One becomes pieces. Somebody got heated.

items.label: Single Replacement: The Homewrecker
items.body: A + BC → AC + B. New element steals the partner. More reactive always wins.

items.label: Double Replacement: Partner Swap
items.body: AB + CD → AD + CB. Two couples switch. The precipitate is the awkwardness becoming visible.

items.label: Combustion: The Bonfire
items.body: Fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + ENERGY. Every campfire, engine, and rocket launch.

blocks.body: The baking soda + vinegar volcano? That's double replacement THEN decomposition. NaHCO₃ + CH₃COOH → NaCH₃COO + H₂CO₃. But H₂CO₃ is unstable — decomposes into H₂O + CO₂ (the fizz). Two reaction types in one volcano.

questions.question: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. What type?

options.B: Synthesis — two elements combine into one compound

questions.explanation: Two reactants (Mg + O₂) → ONE product (MgO). A + B → AB = synthesis.

questions.question: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂. What type?
questions.explanation: Element (Zn) + compound (HCl) → zinc takes hydrogen's place. A + BC → AC + B.

questions.question: C₃H₈ + 5O₂ → 3CO₂ + 4H₂O. How do you recognize combustion?
questions.explanation: Combustion: fuel (C+H compound) + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + energy. Not all O₂ reactions are combustion (rust isn't).

blocks.title: What Equations Really Tell You
blocks.body: A balanced equation gives exact RATIOS. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O means: for every 2 H₂ molecules, you need exactly 1 O₂, and get exactly 2 H₂O.

blocks.title: The Bridge to the Mole
blocks.body: Equations give molecule-level ratios, but you can't count molecules in a lab — you measure grams. The MOLE is the tool that connects molecule ratios (equations) to measurable masses (balance). The mole bridges the equation world and the laboratory world.

Chemical equations describe reactions at the molecular level. But in a lab, you work with grams and liters. The mole concept connects these scales. It's the most powerful — and initially most confusing — concept in intro chemistry. We'll take it step by careful step.

blocks.title: The Recipe Analogy
blocks.body: A balanced equation is a recipe. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O is like '2 cups flour + 1 egg → 2 cookies.' Coefficients are ratios. Want 4 cookies? Double everything.

But molecules are unimaginably small. You can't count '2 molecules of hydrogen.' You need a way to measure molecular quantities with a scale. The mole solves this — a counting unit so large that molecular ratios become gram-scale measurements.
Think of the mole like 'dozen' but for atoms. A dozen = 12. A mole = 6.02 × 10²³. Absurdly large because atoms are absurdly small. Next lesson unlocks all of quantitative chemistry.

blocks.title: Equations Are Recipes
blocks.body: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. 'Take 1 methane, add 2 oxygens, get 1 CO₂ and 2 waters.' Coefficients are the recipe. Scale up, ratios stay the same.

Problem: you can't weigh '2 molecules of oxygen.' They're too tiny. You need a number so big that a batch of molecules weighs enough to measure. That number is 6.02 × 10²³ — called a mole. It's the most important number in chemistry. And it's going to make perfect sense.

Coming next: the mole concept. The idea that terrifies chemistry students. The number that seems too big to be real. Don't worry — we'll build it step by step, and by the end you'll wonder why everyone said it was hard.

questions.question: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O gives a 2:1 ratio. If you had 6 H₂ molecules, how many O₂ needed?
questions.explanation: 2 H₂ : 1 O₂. Triple to 6 H₂ → triple O₂ to 3. Ratios scale proportionally. This is the foundation of stoichiometry.
